title: Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)
app: Native OS or universal forwarder performance inputs, Telegraf → Performance CIM
spl:
index=perf tag=endpoint metric_name="cpu_pct"
| bin _time span=5m
| stats perc95(value) as cpu_p95 by _time, host
| where cpu_p95>90
description: Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware — use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).
value: Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware — use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).
implementation: Prefer `mstats` for metrics indexes; join CIM `Performance.host` to CMDB tier.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Performance.CPU by Performance.host span=5m | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: High resource use from backups, A/V full scans, software deployment, and scheduled reporting jobs; time-of-day baselines and maintenance windows catch most of this.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: native OS metrics, Telegraf → Performance CIM.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Normalized performance metrics per `host`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Prefer `mstats` for metrics indexes; join CIM `Performance.host` to CMDB tier.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=perf tag=endpoint metric_name="cpu_pct"
| bin _time span=5m
| stats perc95(value) as cpu_p95 by _time, host
| where cpu_p95>90
```

Understanding this SPL

**Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)** — Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware — use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).

Documented **Data sources**: Normalized performance metrics per `host`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): native OS metrics, Telegraf → Performance CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: perf.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=perf, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where cpu_p95>90` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Performance.CPU by Performance.host span=5m | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)** — Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware — use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).

Documented **Data sources**: Normalized performance metrics per `host`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): native OS metrics, Telegraf → Performance CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Performance.CPU` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Step 3 — Validate
Accelerate the CIM data model used in the optional tstats path, then run both the primary and `cimSpl` searches over a week that includes a known good baseline. Spot-check a few rows and tune thresholds; confirm no permission or tagging gap is hiding part of the population.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (CPU p95), Heatmap (host × time).
grandmaExplanation: We use the same kind of check described for "Endpoint Resource Utilization" once our data is shaped in a common, repeatable way across products. We help the team see trouble early, compare different tools fairly, and avoid building a one-off report for every vendor.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Performance.CPU` dataset after normalizing this feed to the model; otherwise the `cimSpl` tstats search may return no rows.
